does: This paper investigates catastrophic forgetting in fine-tuned language models by proposing that models implicitly infer task identity from prompts, causing fine-tuning to shift task inference toward the fine-tuning distribution at the expense of other capabilities. The authors introduce Conjugate Prompting, a technique that reformulates prompts to appear farther from the fine-tuning distribution while preserving the underlying capability needed. In controlled experiments, they demonstrate that conjugate prompting recovers lost pretraining capabilities. They then apply this insight to real-world instruction-tuned language models by translating prompts into languages underrepresented in the fine-tuning data, successfully recovering in-context learning and other capabilities that instruction tuning had diminished. The approach is simple, requiring only prompt modification without model retraining.
connects: This work builds on longstanding concerns about catastrophic forgetting in neural networks, particularly acute in language models where fine-tuning for specific tasks often degrades performance on held-out abilities. It extends recent investigations into how instruction tuning and RLHF affect model capabilities beyond their target distribution. The paper connects to research on task inference and prompt sensitivity in large language models, as well as work examining how models parse and understand task structure from context. It also relates to findings on language-agnostic representations in multilingual models and how pretraining knowledge can be preserved or recovered through prompting strategies. The implicit inference hypothesis draws on understanding of how models generalize across task boundaries.
enables: This work opens directions for better understanding and mitigating capability degradation during fine-tuning, suggesting that task structure inference is a key mechanism affecting generalization. It enables simpler, training-free methods to recover lost capabilities in deployed models through prompt engineering, which has immediate practical applications for improving instruction-tuned language models without expensive retraining. The conjugate prompting framework could motivate broader investigation into how prompting can align model behavior with different task representations. Future work could explore whether this principle extends to other distribution shifts beyond language, develop more sophisticated task-obfuscation methods, and investigate the relationship between task inference and safety properties like alignment, potentially revealing tensions between fine-tuning for helpfulness and maintaining broad capabilities.
